# Transcrição: 02-What Financials You Need and How to Ask for Them

- **Fonte:** 02-What Financials You Need and How to Ask for Them
- **Duração:** 08:50
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Welcome back. In this module we will discuss the three major financial statements you'll need to request from prospective businesses, the importance of a general ledger, and statement timeframes. And finally, we'll talk about audited and unaudited statements. So let's jump right in. There are three essential financial statements you'll need in order to evaluate a prospective business, the value of that business, and perform the financial due diligence which we'll discuss in the later module. You'll need income statements, also called profit and loss statements or P&L for short. You'll need balance sheets and cash flow statements. In the next module I'll walk you through everything you need to know about each of these documents and how to interpret them. But before we get there, I also want to discuss the general ledgers. General ledgers are essentially the bookkeeping system for any given company. It provides a record of each financial transaction that takes place during the life of the company. Think of it like you would the transaction history in your checking account. It shows all debits and credits on the company's books. Typically, if a company has a good accountant, you'll see these transactions organized into categories and accounts that logically match the income statement. Why is this important? Because as you dig into the financial statements for prospective companies, you'll inevitably have questions about revenue or expenses. For example, you might notice that marketing expenses for a company spike up randomly one month versus another. You could easily ask the business owner for the explanation. And they very likely can explain the variability in costs. However, it's your job at a certain point to not only ask, but verify. And this is where the general ledger comes in handy. You should be able to pull up the general ledger, drill down to all expenses categorized as marketing, and verify exactly what was spent to where, to who, to when, and to why. In this way, you can often answer most of your own questions with a copy of the general ledger. Some companies include the general ledger as an extra tab in the income statement spreadsheet. But some do not. If it's not included, we recommend that you request a copy for all financial statement timeframes that you've been given. And that's a great segue into my next point. Timeframes. When you request all three types of financial statements from the company owner and or their accountants, ensure that you are specific that they must all be from the timeframes you requested or as close as possible. Final income statements are typically produced at the end of a company's fiscal year. This can vary and

## [03:00 - 06:00]

is not always tied to the calendar year. You should request income statements for a minimum of the past three years, plus an interim income statement for the current fiscal year that might not yet be closed. Balance sheet should also reflect the fiscal year and the cash flow statement should ideally be pulled and dated for the last day of the timeframe reflected on the balance sheet. This will give you a clear picture into the profitability, the assets, the liabilities, the cash liquidity of the business over a similar and comparable timeframe. You'll ensure the clearest picture of the company's financial health if you follow these recommendations. Even if you get all the statements, a general ledger and in the same timeframe, you still don't really know the reliability and the information you've been given. And this is why it's important to understand audited versus unaudited statements. Some people will tell you not to worry about this until later in the buying process. But I disagree. I believe it's important to perform a cursory level of accounting due diligence at the beginning. You're establishing a baseline of reliability of the financial statements you'll use to craft your offer, value the business and close the deal. So the first most obvious question to ask is who's their accountants? Then do a little online digging to establish the accountants rank and status in the accounting world. At the very top of the accounting pyramid in the United States are four big firms, Deloitte, Ernst & Young, Price Waterhouse Coopers and KPMG. It's very likely the company you're looking at will use similar sized firms. But more often than not, I call what they use shoebox accounting. They take their receipts, they drop them into a shoebox, they take their revenue, they deposit it in the bank. So having a good accountant on your side to formulate and build this is very important. It also tells you the opportunity. If you get the tax returns of a business and they show they have high revenues and they have crappy accounting, then they're paying taxes so they're pretty certain they're generating that revenue. But if they're not doing accounting, this means there could be huge upside in that business. Nothing against these people personally, but as a potential buyer, it just puts up some red flags for the consistency and integrity of financial statements. Furthermore, you want to ask if the statements have been audited or not. Most small businesses will never have audited statements. If they're big businesses, they may have audited statements and usually if they have partners involved. Audited statements, as you'd suspect, have been thoroughly reviewed and vetted by trained, incredible accountants. In the end, they come up with an auditor's report. And in this report, you'll ideally be looking for an unqualified opinion or a clean opinion. This means the financial statements are presumed to be free from material misstatements and mistakes. But I'm going to tell you, you can't always rely on that either.

## [06:01 - 08:50]

This is one of the most important parts of getting the deal done, understanding and reconstructing the financials. This is critically important, so having a great accountant is equally important to getting a quality deal done. Now, if a company you're looking to acquire has a clean opinion from a reputable accounting firm, you can be very confident in the statements you're about to review. If not, don't worry. I mean, honestly, it is so uncommon for a small mom and pop business to have their books audited. It's even more uncommon to see them have great accounting. So the fact is, this creates an opportunity for you as a buyer. What's most important, though, is to ask the necessary questions to establish some sort of baseline of reliability before you get started. Finally, the last question you should ask the business owner or accountant when requesting financial statements is if the company is run on a cruel or cash-based accounting. This is a very important piece of information. A cruel accounting is a financial accounting method that allows a company to record revenue before receiving payments for goods or services sold. Similarly, expenses are recorded as incurred before the company has been paid for them. In other words, the revenue earned is recognized on the company's accounting books regardless of when cash transactions have occurred. This typically is a preferred accounting method for most businesses. A cruel accounting generally makes the relationship between revenue and expenses clear, providing a better insight into profitability. It also offers a more accurate picture of the company's assets and liabilities on its balance sheet. The alternative, cash-based accounting, reflects only the true revenue and expenses in and out of the business in a given statement timeframe. For example, month to month. If you receive revenue and you pay a bill in a month, that is cash accounting the way you document that. What this ultimately means is cash in and bills paid on a month-to-month basis may not be totally inlined, but I like looking at cash-basis businesses because it shows me how much cash is actually coming into the business. The clear connection and correlation between income and cost is lost in this method. As I said in the next lesson, we'll dig into each of these three major statements and understand their structure and how to interpret them because at the end of the day, you want to take the tax returns and all this financial work and you want to compare them so that you can match however they're accounting for their revenue and their costs to what they're actually paying in taxes. I'll see you there.
